parentQuestion: The mushroom poison muscarine can bind to receptors on ________.

scaffolds.q: Muscarine is a toxin found in certain mushrooms.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Some Inocybe and Clitocybe mushrooms contain muscarine, which can cause poisoning.

scaffolds.q: Muscarine was used to NAME muscarinic receptors (they respond to muscarine).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Just like nicotinic receptors respond to nicotine, muscarinic receptors respond to muscarine.

scaffolds.q: Muscarinic receptors are found at parasympathetic TARGET organs.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Heart, GI tract, glands, bladder - parasympathetic effectors have muscarinic receptors.

scaffolds.q: Muscarinic receptors are also found at sweat glands (sympathetic but cholinergic).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Sweat glands are the sympathetic cholinergic exception - they have muscarinic receptors too.

scaffolds.q: Muscarine does NOT bind nicotinic receptors (ganglia, NMJs).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Muscarine is specific for muscarinic receptors. Nicotinic receptors require nicotine or ACh.

scaffolds.q: Muscarine poisoning causes excessive parasympathetic symptoms (SLUDGE).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! SLUDGE = Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis. All muscarinic effects.

scaffolds.q: Atropine (muscarinic blocker) is the antidote for muscarine poisoning.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Atropine blocks muscarinic receptors, counteracting the effects of muscarine.

scaffolds.q: Muscarine binds muscarinic receptors on all postganglionic cholinergic effector cells.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Anywhere postganglionic cholinergic fibers release ACh onto muscarinic receptors, muscarine can also bind.

scaffolds.q: The answer is 'all effector cells stimulated by postganglionic cholinergic fibers.'
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Muscarine binds muscarinic receptors at parasympathetic targets AND sympathetic cholinergic targets (sweat glands). Not ganglia (nicotinic), not NMJs (nicotinic), not adrenal medulla (nicotinic).
